reasoning: The informal statement asserts that "all possible dimension sequences are EXACTLY the sequences of the form ... for some k ∈ ℕ ∪ {+∞}", which is a set equality requiring both (1) every 1-dim ring gives such a sequence, and (2) every such k ∈ ℕ ∪ {+∞} is realized by some 1-dim ring. The Lean formalization has two conjuncts: the first correctly captures direction (1) using ∃ (k : ℕ∞), but the second conjunct uses `∀ (k : ℕ)` — only natural numbers, not ℕ∞. This omits the case k = +∞, which corresponds to the sequence a_n = 2n+1 for all n (since n ≤ ⊤ is always true in the definition of `a`). Thus the Lean statement fails to assert that this "maximal" sequence is realized, making it strictly weaker than the informal claim: any proof of the informal statement yields the Lean one, but the Lean one could be true even if no 1-dim ring achieved the k = +∞ sequence.
